data.title: Hong Kong records 10% rise in festive period visitors as outbound trips surge 22%
data.description:
Hong Kong received 10 per cent more visitors during the Christmas period compared with last year, while Hongkongers made almost 1.7 million outbound trips, up 22 per cent over the same period, according to official statistics.
However, the retail sector warned that the absence of New Year fireworks could affect the number of visitors in the coming days, further dampening local consumption sentiment following the deadly fire in Tai Po.
“Because of the Tai Po fire, consumer sentiment weakened over the past three weeks, affecting overall retail sales,” said lawmaker Peter Shiu Ka-fai, who represents the retail sector.
“Coupled with the trend of residents heading north to spend on the mainland, I believe retail performance [over the Christmas period] may not be as strong as last year.”
The city experienced a more subdued festive atmosphere after Hong Kong’s deadliest blaze in decades last month, which killed at least 161 residents.
Shiu said that while sales of essential products such as cosmetics appeared stronger, overall Christmas spending fell short of last year’s levels.
He added that the cancellation of the New Year’s Eve fireworks display at Victoria Harbour in the wake of the deadly blaze could further affect tourist arrivals and in turn retail sales.
Nonetheless, Shiu expected sales to rebound during the Lunar New Year, when he believed consumer sentiment would recover.
According to the Immigration Department, Hong Kong recorded 404,559 inbound visitor trips from December 24 to 26.
Of these, 261,526 were from mainland China, up 6 per cent from last year, while non-mainland visitors accounted for 143,033, a 16 per cent increase.
During the same period, a total of 3,592,601 cross-border trips were recorded, up 10 per cent from 2024. Of these, 1,691,392 were outbound trips by Hongkongers, compared with 1,382,523 the previous year – an increase of 22 per cent.
Breaking down the figures, Boxing Day saw 436,025 departing and 498,893 returning trips by Hong Kong residents. Christmas Eve and Christmas Day recorded a further 590,707 and 664,660 outbound trips respectively.
Almost 90 per cent of Hongkongers’ outbound trips, or 1,493,546, were made via border control points connecting the city with the mainland, underscoring residents’ continuing northbound spending trend.
The busiest control points were Lo Wu and Lok Ma Chau Spur Line, with 352,030 and 309,575 outbound trips respectively, up 17 per cent and 12 per cent year on year. Another 171,120 trips were by air, a 14 per cent increase.
Tens of thousands of people were stuck for hours at the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge early on Christmas Day, queuing for at least 1½ hours to board the cross-border Gold Bus.

On Saturday, as of 4pm, a total of 615,793 cross-border trips had been made, including more than 250,000 by Hong Kong residents leaving the city and almost 160,000 by inbound visitors.
Timothy Chui Ting-pong, executive director of the Hong Kong Tourism Association, said outbound tour group sales rose 10 per cent year on year over the Christmas period.
“Business during Christmas has been quite good,” Chui said. “It reflects an improvement in the economic situation in the second half of this year, so residents take the opportunity of the long holiday to travel.”
Chui, who was leading a tour group to Guangxi province and Vietnam, said itineraries that incorporated high-speed rail were especially popular, including those to Fujian, Xiamen and Yunnan.
Separately, Chui attributed the growth in inbound tourism to increased arrivals from Southeast Asia and Japan.
He noted that visitors from Southeast Asia could enjoy more Christmas activities and a stronger festive atmosphere in Hong Kong than in their home countries, while Japanese tourists were drawn by the martial arts film Twilight of the Warriors: Walled In, which screened last year. The film, featuring the demolished Kowloon Walled City, gained significant popularity in Japan.
Alan Chan Chung-yee, chief operations officer of Miramar Group, and Jack Cheung Ki-tang, director of CTS HK Metropark Hotels Management, both said on Friday their companies’ hotels had occupancy rates of 90 per cent or above.
Simon Wong Kit-lung, chairman of the LH Group of restaurants, said sales at his eateries during the Christmas period were similar to last year’s, despite more residents being out of town.
“From my observations, the shopping malls were bustling this year. Many of my restaurants saw customers queuing for tables even after 9pm during the holidays, especially in tourist areas,” Wong said.

data.title: China hits milestone in building Beishan laboratory for managing nuclear waste
data.description:
China National Nuclear Corporation (CNNC) on Friday announced a major milestone in the construction of one of the world’s largest underground laboratories for nuclear waste as it completed a first-of-its-kind spiral ramp.
Located deep in the Gobi Desert northwest of Jiuquan in Gansu province, the Beishan Underground Research Laboratory aims to address a fundamental challenge of nuclear energy development: the safe disposal of radioactive waste.
“While nuclear energy is a clean and efficient power source, about 99 per cent of the radioactive waste it produces is classified as low- or intermediate-level, decaying to harmless levels over time,” said Wang Ju, chief scientist at CNNC and the laboratory’s chief designer, in a company report.
“However, the remaining 1 per cent constitutes high-level waste, which requires safe disposal and isolation for hundreds of thousands of years.”
The best solution is to bury that high-level waste 500 to 1,000 metres (1,640 to 3,280 feet) underground, ensuring isolation. This is often called “the final link in the nuclear industry chain”.
“Every country has its own unique strategies for managing nuclear waste,” Wang said. “Through the Beishan platform, we will exchange knowledge – bringing in global best practices and sharing our own findings with the world.”
Building such a facility, however, is immensely challenging. It requires excavating a cavity within a massive solid rock body.
The decision to build the laboratory in the remote, sparsely populated Beishan area of Gansu, known for its rock formations, came after nearly 30 years of research. Starting in 1996, researchers drilled nearly 100 boreholes to understand the complex geology of the area.
After receiving approval from nuclear energy authorities in 2019, construction formally began in June 2021.

The laboratory’s main structure features an innovative layout consisting of a spiral access ramp, three vertical shafts and two horizontal levels reaching a maximum depth of 560 metres.
The recently completed spiral ramp is a 7.03-metre-diameter tunnel stretching around 7km (4.3 miles) and descending at a 10 per cent gradient. Its construction posed huge challenges.
The first was the extremely hard rock at the site, which meant traditional excavation methods were ineffective and damaging to the surrounding rock. The subsurface at Beishan consists primarily of intact granite formed 260 million years ago.
The tight turns of the spiral ramp also presented a daunting challenge for the 100-metre boring machine – akin to asking an elephant to turn nimbly in a confined space.
The steep slope of the ramp placed extreme demands on the boring machine’s climbing ability, muck removal system and directional control.
Finally, the laboratory’s depth, reaching 560 metres below the surface, created significant safety risks during construction.
The Beishan No 1 boring machine used in the construction is a first-of-its-kind machine independently developed by China for hard rock and steep, curved tunnels. Key developers of the machine included the Beijing Research Institute of Uranium Geology and China Railway Construction Heavy Industry Corporation.
During construction, the team limited damage to the surrounding rock to within a few centimetres. This level of precision is crucial for making sure the laboratory is safe and leakproof.
Construction progressed alongside scientific experiments, with over 40 domestic and international research institutes and university teams carrying out large-scale engineering tests.
These led to innovations in breaking rock efficiently, high-performance boring, muck removal during tunnelling and precise three-dimensional directional control for spiral boring.

data.title: Elderly man unconscious after being hit by HK$2.3 million Porsche in Hong Kong
data.description:
An elderly man has been rushed to hospital in Hong Kong after being struck by a HK$2.3 million (US$295,900) Porsche sports car.
Police said on Saturday they received a report at about 2.23pm of a private vehicle near the junction of Tai Kiu Road and Yuen Long On Lok Road that had apparently lost control and crashed into a man in his seventies.
Emergency personnel were deployed to the scene and the man was rushed to Tuen Mun Hospital in an unconscious state, the force said. Information about the driver was not immediately available.
According to images from the scene, the car is a Porsche 718 Cayman GT4 RS. The vehicle appeared to have crashed through a barricade while making a turn, and most of its front end was damaged.
According to Porsche’s website, the vehicle is advertised as being “highly agile” with prices starting from HK$2.3 million.
Police said an investigation into the accident was still under way.

data.title: Hong Kong police arrest 2 more in connection with murder of man in August
data.description:
Hong Kong police have apprehended two people at Shenzhen Bay Port in connection with the murder of a 51-year-old man in August, bringing the number of arrestees linked to the case up to 27.
The force said on Saturday that the pair, two men aged 29 and 32, were arrested a day earlier at the border crossing on suspicion of murder.
Friday’s arrests were linked to the murder of a man in Tin Shui Wai on August 25. The victim’s body was discovered with a knife wound to the head under a bridge over Yick Yuen Road.
A source previously said investigators had identified him as Zeng Pengshan, a Hong Kong identity card holder.
The Post also earlier learned that the victim was a construction worker who was believed to have helped deliver illicit cigarettes before making off with HK$4 million (US$514,400) worth of product after failing to get a promised reward.
Police in August said the victim had been abducted and was injured when he attempted to escape, dying as he was taken away in his own car before his body was dumped under the bridge.
A passer-by, who was on the way to work, discovered the victim unconscious and covered in blood at the scene. No suspicious individuals were seen at the location at the time.
The latest arrests bring the number of people apprehended in connection with the case up to 27.
Police said on Saturday that holding charges of murder, conspiracy to murder, assisting offenders and perverting the course of justice had been laid against 11 of the suspects.
Some of the cases were mentioned at Fanling Court between August and November, with 14 of the suspects being released on bail and two being detained for further inquiries, the force added.
Police took one of the suspects arrested on Friday back to the scene for a re-enactment. He was seen in the company of two officers and wearing a grey hoodie with his face covered.
The re-enactment involved a mannequin and a car, with a videographer documenting the process as the group walked around the site.
The force’s New Territories North regional crime unit is handling the case. Anyone with information relevant to the investigation has been urged to contact officers at 3661 3362.

data.title: Kyiv strike shows Russia does ‘not want to end war’, Zelensky warns ahead of US talks
data.description:
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said on Saturday that a Russian drone and missile attack on Kyiv showed Moscow did “not want to end the war”.
Zelensky made the comments ahead of his departure for talks in the United States with President Donald Trump on a plan to halt the Russian invasion.
The Russians “do not want to end the war and seek to use every opportunity to cause Ukraine even greater suffering and increase their pressure on others around the world”, Zelensky said.
Russia attacked the Ukrainian capital with nearly 500 drones and 40 missiles overnight, targeting energy and civilian infrastructure, the president said.
At least one person died and 11 people were injured in the attack, which affected seven locations across Kyiv. Two children were among the wounded, Kyiv’s City Military Administration head Tymur Tkachenko said in a statement on Telegram.

A fire broke out in an 18-storey residential building in the Dnipro district of the city, and emergency crews rushed to the scene to contain the flames.
Tkachenko said a 24-storey residential building in the Darnytsia district was also hit and more fires broke out in the Obolonskyi and Holosiivsky districts.
In the wider Kyiv region, the strike hit industrial and residential buildings, according to Ukraine’s Emergency Service. In the Vyshhorod area, emergency crews rescued one person found under the rubble of a destroyed house.
The attack left a ‌third of ‍Kyiv without heating, Ukraine’s foreign minister Andrii Sybiha said on Saturday.
“Russia’s only response to peace efforts is brutal attacks using hundreds of drones and missiles against Kyiv and other cities and regions,” Sybiha wrote on social media.
The temperature in the capital hovered around 0 degrees Celsius (32 degrees Fahrenheit) on Saturday.
“If Russia turns even the Christmas and ‍New Year period into a time of destroyed homes and burned ‍apartments, of ruined power plants, then this sick activity can only be responded to with truly strong steps,” Zelensky wrote on social media, calling for the US and Europe to pressure Moscow harder.
Additional reporting by Reuters, Associated Press

data.title: China woman shocked to find male therapist at spa appointment, is mocked for being ‘ignorant’
data.description:
A woman in central China who was lying naked while awaiting a massage at a spa was shocked when a male therapist appeared, and she refused his services.
Following her refusal she was mocked by the manager of the spa for being “ignorant”.
The incident unfolded on December 12 at Yushiman Hi-tech Anti-ageing Centre in Changsha, Hunan province.
The customer, surnamed Huang, undressed and lay on her stomach on a bed in anticipation of a shoulder and neck massage, Fengmang News reported.

Huang said she heard a female manager come into her room to place a towel over her.
Then another person came in and Huang immediately realised it was a man.
“I was naked at that time. So I said I did not accept a man to serve me. The female manager asked me why I could not accept a male therapist. I said, why should I accept a male therapist when I was naked,” Huang was quoted as saying.
“The manager told me not to be so ignorant. She said there are also male doctors at hospitals and male therapists at foot massage parlours,” said Huang.
She threatened to call the police during the altercation.

The manager agreed to change a therapist, but Huang said she was no longer in the mood for a massage.
“What the manager said made me uncomfortable. She thought I was making a big deal out of it. She said I was ignorant and that the male worker was handsome and in his 20s,” said Huang.
“She told me I was old enough to be his mother and as a result he would not be interested in me,” she added.
Huang said that despite the fact that the male therapist did not touch her body, she still regarded it as an offence because he entered the room without her permission.
The manager said they sent a man to attend to Huang because she did not specify which gender she wanted.

Finally, the manager apologised to Huang.
“Initially I did not think I was wrong. In many spas, it is common practice that male therapists serve female customers, while females massage the males,” said the unidentified manager.
The story went viral on mainland social media.
“The spa was wrong. They should check with the customer first. Also, their attitude was unpleasant,” said one online observer.
But another person said: “My understanding is that massage centres send therapists to customers of the opposite gender. Otherwise, customers will get angry.”

data.title: What’s next for Katseye? The ‘global girl group’ on their Beautiful Chaos Tour and reclaiming their lives as 20-year-olds
data.description:
Almost every Katseye performance begins the same way. The bass notes of the girl group’s first single “Debut” echo through the venue, bright stage lights shine on its members and a screaming crowd nearly drowns out their vocals.
But roughly a week before heading out on their first official tour, their opening number was met with a few sparse claps.

Inside the Hollywood Palladium, the group – fresh-faced, high-spirited and decked out in their colour-coordinated stagewear – held their final dress rehearsal last month. It was both the first time they’d run the show on a stage and the last time they would practice before the sold-out Beautiful Chaos Tour.
Their final stop on the North American trek was in Los Angeles, where they played the YouTube Theater and the Palladium on December 12 and 13.
The global girl group, made up of Megan Skiendiel, Sophia Laforteza, Daniela Avanzini, Lara Raj, Yoonchae Jeung and Manon Bannerman, is in the midst of a rapid rise to fame. In a matter of months – since the release of their second EP, Beautiful Chaos, in June – they have continued to make each of their pop star dreams come true, with anything from the viral Gap campaign, a record-breaking crowd at Lollapalooza and two Grammy nominations.

The group originated from a YouTube reality competition show called The Debut: Dream Academy in 2023. Taking influence from the K-pop training system, over 120,000 contestants competed for a spot in what would become Katseye. The show was presented by Hybe, the South Korean entertainment company behind acts like BTS and Le Sserafim, and Geffen, a label of Universal Music, which shared the goal of crafting a multicultural girl group. (Avanzini is Venezuelan-Cuban; Raj is Indian; Skiendiel is Chinese Singaporean; Laforteza is Filipina; Bannerman is Swiss-Ghanaian; and Jeung is Korean.)

Their on-screen journey was simultaneously captured through the Netflix docuseries, Popstar Academy: Katseye, which took viewers inside their intense training and evaluations.
Two years after their debut, Katseye remains in a similar situation – where the stakes haven’t disappeared, but rather changed. They are no longer competing for a spot in the group; they are instead navigating their swift rise to stardom.
Fresh off the stage of the nearly empty venue, Katseye traded their Y2K-inspired performance looks for sweats and sat around a coffee table, indulging in late-night snacks. In between fits of laughter and finishing each other’s sentences, the members explain that they’d recently come back from a European press tour and only had a week to nail down their tour performance.
It sounds like you have been operating on crunch time for these upcoming shows. Do you usually have more time to prepare?

Raj: With every performance that we’ve ever done, we’ve always had a really extensive amount of rehearsal – sometimes a little too much rehearsal even. It takes a lot of time to make sure things are so synchronised and super sharp. This was our first time putting something together so fast.
Laforteza: Because of our growth and how fast everything is happening, we are still learning to become more seasoned and prepared in short amounts of time. It’s what all our previous training is for. Now we’re starting to figure out how to do a lot more things at once. We’re almost everywhere now. We’re all over the internet. We’re getting these nominations. We’re showing up to all these events, so we have a lot less time to prepare for certain things.
Katseye is in the middle of its biggest year so far. What’s it like being on the inside of that whirlwind?

Skiendiel: I’m still trying to grasp onto the feeling of being in Katseye. We got nominated for two Grammys a couple of days ago, and that was so exciting, but I was so focused on these tour rehearsals that I didn’t even really get to process it fully. You know, what’s actually crazy – yesterday we had an off day, and I was watching one of our older interviews. We said we wanted a VMA, a Grammy, to play Coachella and do a tour. Since then, we’ve won a VMA, received Grammy nominations, will play Coachella next year and are about to leave on tour.
It seems like you all have some kind of manifesting power. How does it feel to have chosen this pop star life and have it progress so quickly?

Avanzini: When we were all trainees, all of our goals were the same. Over these past three years, we see how much we’ve pushed ourselves and how resilient we can be. Now that we are currently accomplishing the goals we set out to do, it makes us want to think of new goals to accomplish.
Sometimes, though, we get so lost. All we see in our daily lives is travelling, rehearsals and shows. We get so caught up in our schedules.
Raj: We’ll often forget to reflect on and enjoy what’s happening. The only time I’m aware of what’s really happening is when we’re performing. There’s this moment of being on stage, and being aware of every single person.
What happens when this mindfulness takes over?

Avanzini: For me, I’m always so stressed out before [a show]. When I first started performing, I would just black out and do the performance. Then I’d get offstage and have no idea what happened.
But now we’re more comfortable on stage. It feels like home, especially because we’re with each other. It’s not as scary as being a soloist. It’s nice to be able to look around on stage and give each other a smile. It grounds me a lot.
When you were on stage performing in an empty venue, just a few minutes ago, it still seemed like you were having the time of your lives. What keeps you going, even after practising all day?

Laforteza: Passion!
[Each member audibly agrees between bites of Reese’s Peanut Butter Cups and Doritos.]
Raj: I f****** love giving a show. There’s nothing more that I love than being in a hot outfit with a fan blowing my hair and dancing. It’s like living in a fantasy. There is nothing more thrilling than that. That’s why we’ll stay here till 3am. We’ve been waiting for this [moment] since we were babies. This show is the entire reason we do it.
Jeung: When I was a K-pop trainee and was having a hard time, I would think of being on the stage with other girls and seeing all the fans. I would draw that picture in my head and want to stay there.
People have been fans of you before you were officially Katseye, back when you were reality TV contestants. You have grown so much since then, welcoming in new aesthetics, hairstyles and music production. What is your relationship to change like?

Skiendiel: The more we grow, the more stories we have to tell. Not all of our music is going to be like “Touch” or “Gnarly”. We’re now having so many new experiences that we’ve gathered from this era that we’re gonna put into our next EP.
Raj: Our personal lives were completely on the back burner for three years before this year. It was completely nonexistent. We didn’t go to parties and we didn’t see anyone. This year, I think we were able to prioritise having life experiences and being 19- and 20-year-olds. Our art is going to be so mundane and robotic if we’re not real people who are going out and trying new things.

Whatever we do next is going to have a lot more soul and offer authentic emotions and experiences. The art we make next will feel more like art rather than just a performance. It’s where we are now as a group. We’ve grown up a bit, and there will be more depth to what we have to say.

data.title: Netflix K-drama Idol I: Choi Soo-young, Kim Jae-young lead K-pop-infused legal drama
data.description:
Lead cast: Choi Soo-young, Kim Jae-young
Latest Nielsen rating: 2.26 per cent
As 2025 draws to a close, viewers are invited back into a Korean courtroom one last time with Idol I (also styled as I Dol I). This time you can leave your lawbooks behind, since the emphasis is squarely on character melodrama rather than legal procedure.
Choi Soo-young (Second Shot at Love) and Kim Jae-young (The Judge from Hell) team up as a lawyer and a K-pop star, respectively.


Choi – herself a member of the K-pop supergroup Girls’ Generation – plays Maeng Se-na, a young partner at her firm known for winning unwinnable cases that others refuse to touch.
Her latest challenge involves Do Ra-ik (Kim), a solo idol accused of murdering his friend and former bandmate, Kang Woo-sung (Ahn Woo-yeon, King the Land).
Se-na is accustomed to high-profile cases and immune to the media scrutiny that accompanies them, no matter how viciously she is criticised. However, this specific trial threatens to crash her professional life into her carefully guarded private world.
Off the clock, Se-na is secretly a devoted K-pop fan whose ultimate idol is none other than Ra-ik himself.

The series dedicates significant screen time to Se-na’s fixation, while simultaneously contrasting it with Ra-ik’s reality: a star struggling with the immense pressure of a solo career, weighed down by the expectations of fans, management and himself alike.
While Se-na the fangirl is fully realised, Se-na the lawyer or even Se-na the person remains a sketch.
After winning a major but vaguely defined case in the opening, she ignores her colleagues’ celebratory gestures upon her return to the office, skipping a victory party in her honour to rush home and obsess over Ra-ik.
Flashbacks reveal that Se-na’s devotion is rooted in trauma. As a bullied teenager contemplating suicide by a riverside, she was saved by the sound of Ra-ik busking nearby.

Her fandom was born from that moment of salvation, though he appears to have no memory of the encounter.
Despite hints at these dramatic underpinnings, the initial episodes largely portray Se-na as a hyper-competent but emotionally vacant automaton. The how of her success is clear, but the why of her personality remains missing.
The series demands viewers take Se-na’s prowess on faith. Her antisocial behaviour is treated as comedy, punctuated by aggressive sound effects, while her legal genius is merely asserted by other characters rather than demonstrated in court.
Momentum builds in the second episode as Se-na agrees to take Ra-ik’s case and the trial begins. She faces off against Kwak Byoung-kyung (Jeong Jae-kwang, A Hundred Memories), an arrogant prosecutor from an elite family.

He turns out to be yet another figure from Se-na’s past who does not recognise her: her school bully.
Also briefly introduced is Choi Hee-jin (Heavenly Ever After) as the mysterious Hong Hye-joo, who repeatedly crosses Ra-ik’s path, catching his attention at various events.
Although framed as a legal series, Idol I shows no ambition to be the next Extraordinary Attorney Woo. Its focus lies in character drama, though the execution remains lopsided for the moment.
Ra-ik has the contours of a character, albeit a thin one, while Se-na feels like a collection of tics and triggers. Despite Choi’s spirited performance, the writing has yet to lift the character off the page.

One hopes that as the story evolves, the leads will elevate each other – and that the procedural elements will rise to meet the potential of the premise.
Idol I is streaming on Netflix.

data.title: Supermaxis Comanche, LawConnect continue duel in Sydney to Hobart race, Hong Kong boat 3rd
data.description:
Supermaxis Master Lock Comanche and defending champion LawConnect continued their close duel on Saturday on the second day of the 80th running of the Sydney to Hobart yacht race.
Just over 27 hours into the race that began on Friday in Sydney Harbour and ends on the island state of Tasmania, Comanche had a four-nautical-mile lead over LawConnect.
LawConnect was forced to contend with a broken mainsheet and halyard overnight but managed to fix both issues and remain in pursuit of Comanche, which is skippered by Matt Allen and James Mayo.
“We had a few things slow us down and Comanche’s obviously done quite well,” LawConnect skipper Christian Beck said on Saturday. “It’s mildly disappointing for us, but you win some, lose some. Everything’s good, we’re still in the hunt.”
With more than half the race completed, Hong Kong yacht SHK Scallywag 100 was third, followed closely by the 88-foot Lucky, a New York Yacht Club entry skippered by Bryon Ehrhart.

The leading yachts were in Bass Strait, which separates the mainland from Tasmania. They were likely to finish at Constitution Dock in Hobart late on Sunday afternoon under current weather and wind projections – more than two days after the start and well outside the race record.
The race lost its first supermaxi when Wild Thing 100 retired. All crew members were safe as Wild Thing began the journey back to Sydney after reporting rigging damage.
Skipper Robert Large aboard his yacht Inukshuk was also among the 11 retirements after becoming entangled in a fishing net.
“We couldn’t move. It took us probably 45 minutes to clear the thing,” Large said. After finally pulling the net off, Large was concerned his boat may have sustained rudder damage, which would have made it risky to continue sailing.
An Australian resident for almost 20 years, Canadian-born Large has sailed in six previous Sydney-Hobart races.
LawConnect led the fleet out of Sydney harbour in search of its third straight line honours win. Overnight conditions on Friday night were rough on the fleet, but not as dangerous as last year when two sailors died in storms on the first night.
On Friday, the fleet paid tribute to the victims of the December 14 terror attack by scattering rose petals off the coast of Bondi Beach, off Sydney, as they passed the area early in the race.
LawConnect, owned by Australian tech millionaire Beck, won last year’s event in one day, 13 hours, 35 minutes and 13 seconds for the 628-nautical-mile (722-mile, 1,160km) race.
The race record set by LDV Comanche – one day, nine hours, 15 minutes and 24 seconds – has stood since 2017 and only comes under threat in very strong downwind conditions.

data.title: Indonesian rescuers search for Spanish family of 4 after tourist boat sinks
data.description:
A Spanish family of four is missing after a tourist boat sank in rough seas in Indonesia, state news agency Antara reported on Saturday.
The vessel was carrying 11 people when it sank in the Padar Island Strait near Labuan Bajo on Friday night, Antara said. The popular tourist destination is the main gateway to Komodo National Park, a Unesco World Heritage site.
Seven people, including two Spanish tourists, four crew members and a tour guide, were rescued.
A search for the remaining four was continuing on Saturday, with Padar Island closed to tourists due to extreme weather.
Those missing have been identified as Martin Carreras Fernando, Martin Garcia Mateo, Martines Ortuno Maria Lia and Martinez Ortuno Enriquejavier, according to the Jakarta Globe.
“The four victims are foreign tourists, Spanish citizens, who based on initial data were travelling as one family group,” the newspaper reported Labuan Bajo port authority chief Stephanus Risdiyanto as saying on Saturday.

According to authorities, the KM Putri Sakinah left Komodo Island for Padar Island at around 8pm on Friday for a sightseeing trip. About 30 minutes later, the traditional passenger boat reportedly suffered engine failure and sank.
The Labuan Bajo port authority attributed the sinking to high waves of up to 3 metres (10 feet). “This made it difficult for us to carry out the initial search,” Stephanus told Antara.
Video released by the National Search and Rescue Agency showed emergency workers in blue uniforms helping people, some barefoot, out of an inflatable orange boat and onto shore.
Other footage showed people in life jackets sitting on a rescue boat, and a similar vessel kicking up spray as it motored across the waves in the dark.
“Search operations are still ongoing, with priority given to the safety of [rescue] personnel and careful consideration of weather and sea conditions,” Stephanus said, according to the Jakarta Globe.
The island’s temporary closure was prompted by the accident and weather monitoring reports, he said.
“Last night, we issued a notice to mariners temporarily closing cruises to Komodo and Padar,” he told Antara, adding that the sailing permit service for the Padar-Komodo route would remain suspended until conditions improved.
Marine accidents occur regularly in Indonesia, a Southeast Asian archipelago of around 17,000 islands, often due to lax safety standards and bad weather.
Additional reporting by SCMP’s Asia desk
